title: A No-Cloning Trade-off Between Black Hole No-Hair and Horizon Smoothness
abstract: The black hole no-hair theorem is traditionally derived from the uniqueness theorems of general relativity. We show that a quantitative form follows from unitarity together with the standard semiclassical assumptions of horizon causality and interior accessibility. For a semiclassical black hole, we prove that the trace distance between exterior states corresponding to two same-charge infalling states is bounded by $2\sqrt{2\varepsilon}$, where $\varepsilon$ quantifies the diamond norm departure of the interior channel from a perfect isometry which is a quantitative measure of horizon-smoothness violation that upper-bounds $1 - F_I$, where $F_I$ is the interior fidelity capturing how faithfully the infalling state is retained. Inverting this relation yields a trade-off inequality, $\varepsilon \geq D_{\max}^2/8$, between the maximum exterior distinguishability $D_{\max}$ and the degree of horizon smoothness. This establishes that observable exterior quantum hair is quantitatively incompatible with exact horizon smoothness under unitary evolution: any model predicting nonzero exterior hair must violate the equivalence principle at the horizon by a quantifiable amount. Pre-existing entanglement with the infalling system is the only channel for quantum hair compatible with both unitarity and horizon smoothness.
summary: This paper establishes a fundamental mathematical trade-off between the ability to distinguish black hole microstates from the outside and the smoothness of the event horizon. It proves that observable quantum hair is quantitatively incompatible with a smooth horizon, meaning any 'hair' requires a violation of the equivalence principle. This provides a rigorous information-theoretic constraint on various black hole microstate models like fuzzballs and soft hair.
main_problem: Investigating the fundamental compatibility between the existence of observable quantum hair (exterior distinguishability) and the smoothness of the black hole event horizon (equivalence principle validity) under unitary evolution.
main_result: The authors derive a quantitative trade-off inequality, epsilon >= D_max^2/8, proving that any model predicting non-zero exterior quantum hair must necessarily involve a violation of horizon smoothness.
method: The study employs quantum information-theoretic tools, specifically the Stinespring dilation of quantum channels, the diamond norm, and the Kretschmann-Schlingemann-Werner (KSW) continuity theorem.
model_or_system: A semiclassical black hole model where the spacetime is partitioned into interior and exterior sectors, treating the horizon crossing as a quantum channel acting on an infalling system.
key_observables: epsilon (horizon smoothness violation/deviation from isometry), D_max (maximum exterior distinguishability/trace distance), and F_I (interior fidelity).
important_parameters: The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy (S_BH) scale, the regime of perfect isometry (epsilon = 0), and the firewall/fuzzball regimes (epsilon ~ O(1)).
main_assumptions: Global unitarity, horizon causality (no signaling from interior to exterior), and diamond-norm interior accessibility.
figures_summary: A Penrose diagram illustrating the black hole interior and exterior regions, showing an infalling system crossing the horizon and the encoding of states.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing an exact no-hair lemma for perfect isometries, then uses the KSW theorem to extend this to an approximate theorem, ultimately deriving the quadratic trade-off inequality and discussing implications for fuzzballs and soft hair.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Penrose diagram of a black hole formed by gravitational collapse. The spacetime is divided into an interior region I (above the dashed horizon line H +) and an exterior region E (below). An infalling quantum system F in state |ψ⟩crosses the horizon, with its state embedded in I via the isometric embedding V . The reduced states ρI(ψ) and ρE(ψ) encode the information accessible to interior and exterior observers, respectively. The trade-off inequality Eq. (16) constrains the relationship between horizon smoothness (ε) and exterior distinguishability (Dmax).
